takeaway: Ramanujam (Monetizing Innovation): how you charge is often more important than how much; have the willingness-to-pay conversation early in development, not after the product is built. 72% of new products launched in the prior five years failed, largely because pricing was an afterthought; build segments around differences in willingness to pay rather than one-size-fits-all.

Curation of Monetizing Innovation's four monetization failure modes: feature shock (cramming too many features into one product), minivation (priced too low to capture full revenue), hidden gem (a strong product never brought to market), and undead (an innovation customers don't want). Core thesis: price should determine the product, not the other way around.

Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter: four questions (too cheap to trust quality / great-value bargain / getting expensive but still considered / too expensive) plot to the Point of Marginal Cheapness, Optimal Price Point, and Point of Marginal Expensiveness. Caveat: intersections can be driven by a small minority of respondents, and PSM works best for single products, not bundles.

Patrick Campbell on the value metric: it is how a customer pays you and what they pay for; align it with revenue brought, cost saved, or efficiency added. If the value metric grows with the customer, you grow from retention alone even without new acquisition.

Value-based pricing framing: your price is an exchange rate on the value you provide. Cites OpenView benchmark that only ~39% of SaaS use value-based pricing vs 24% competitor-based and 10% cost-plus; value-metric companies grow roughly double. Argues against anchoring on competitor pricing.

Comparison of pricing models: per-seat assumes every seat extracts equal value though usage is skewed; usage-based makes buyers ration consumption to manage the bill; flat-rate removes payment friction but cannot capture expansion revenue; most B2B companies converge on hybrid.

Kyle Poyar on the shift beyond seats: when AI does the work, you cannot charge per seat; pricing is a core part of positioning, and success-/outcome-based models align what customers pay with the value they receive.

Poyar on PLG packaging: PLG companies systematically under-monetize as they add value. Heuristic — choose seat-based if value grows as more teammates join; choose usage-based if value is tied directly to feature usage. Make it obvious which plan a buyer belongs in.

Simon-Kucher on decoy pricing and anchoring: the decoy (asymmetric dominance) effect nudges choice toward a more expensive or more profitable option; anchoring means people lean heavily on the first price they see when judging later ones.

Decoy pricing explainer: a third 'decoy' option steers customers toward a higher-priced or more profitable product. Uses the classic Economist three-tier example where print-only and print+web were priced identically to make the bundle look obvious.

Hard paywall vs freemium benchmarks (citing RevenueCat): day-35 download-to-paid ~12.1% hard paywall vs ~2.2% freemium; year-1 LTV ~$49.30 vs ~$24.24. Freemium works when the app has network effects, when users need time to reach value, when optimizing for the long tail, or already at scale.

RevenueCat State of Subscription Apps: the top 25% of apps grew ~80% YoY while the bottom 25% shrank ~33%; 55% of 3-day-trial cancellations happen on day 0; hard paywalls convert far higher than freemium by day 35.

RevenueCat freemium playbook: use paywalls as contextual, dismissible prompts; freemium works when there is a clear upgrade path from free to paid (feature gating, usage limits); the free tier must deliver genuine value, not a crippled trial.

Net Revenue Retention measures how much recurring revenue you retain plus expand from existing customers (upsells, cross-sells, increased usage). NRR of 120% means existing customers generated 20% more this year; a 10-point NRR improvement can translate to a 20–30% increase in valuation.

On the ethics of pricing tests: 65% of customers would feel betrayed to learn they were charged more than others; companies with grandfathering policies during price increases see ~60% less churn; a 5% retention improvement can lift profits 25–95%.

Pricing experimentation methodology: rather than touching core subscription price, many SaaS test add-on or premium-feature pricing; beware confounders like seasonality and Simpson's paradox; cohort-based rollout applies changes to whole cohorts of new customers while grandfathering existing ones.
